title: Continuing Enrollment
description: Intended for graduate students who have completed all necessary credits of course work including thesis (798) and dissertation (899) on their approved Plan of Study, but who have not yet completed and submitted their thesis or dissertation. See the Enrollment Policy for details. The fee for Continuing Enrollment (898) is not eligible for a waiver. Students holding an F-1 or J-1 visa are not eligible to enroll in 898 credits as part of meeting requirements. If a student holding an F-1 or J-1 visa needs to enroll in an 898 course, they must receive prior approval to do so from the international student advisor in Global Programs and Services. Graded S/U. Requires department approval.

title: Medical Terminology for Health Professionals
description: A systematic study of building medical terms and understanding their relationship to human anatomy and physiology, pathology and medical treatment. Pre-CLS, pre-RC, pre-RS, pre-Nursing, pre-Pharmacy majors will receive first preference. Remaining spots, up to the class limit, will then be open to all major programs.

title: Understanding and Developing Compassion in Patient Care
description:
Examination of multiple perspectives on compassion, and reflection on the role of compassion and empathy broadly and in relation to healthcare. Opportunity to apply these concepts through volunteer service in a patient care setting.
Prereq or Coreq: Restricted to Pre-Radiologic Sciences, Pre-Respiratory Care or Pre-Medical Laboratory Science majors only.

title: Critical Thinking, Academic, and Professional Skills for the Health Professions
description:
This course provides students with opportunities to develop proficient critical thinking and clinical problem-solving skills as they are used in the health professions. This course will also immerse students in reasoning-related activities that facilitate academic success, teamwork, and a demonstrated commitment to the roles and responsibilities required of all health professionals.
Cross-listed with PHRM 190 and PH 190 .

title: Beginning Apparel Construction
description: Introduction to basic apparel assembly methods and use of a sewing machine.

title: Apparel Construction and Fit
description:
Principles of apparel construction and analysis. Construction of a fitting sloper and two fashion garments.
Prereq: ARMD 101 .

title: Fashion Dynamics
description: Introductory course tracing the development of fashion and its industry that includes consumer demand and fashion change, the development, production, and marketing of goods from concept to consumer, and their interrelationships.

title: Aesthetics and Visual Analysis of Apparel Products
description: Analysis of aesthetics and design principles and their application to apparel and textiles products, environment and oneself.

title: Sustainability and Social Change in Fashion
description: This course explores the interrelationship between environment, society, economy and culture as they pertain to the global fashion industry. Practical and conceptual issues will be discussed to develop sustainable practices in our daily and professional lives.

title: Dress in World Cultures
description: Analysis of world dress as related to cultural, technological aesthetic, and social patterns. Concepts illustrated through comparative studies of selected world cultures.

title: Fashion Show Production
description:
This course focuses on the planning and execution of fashion shows. Students will gain hands-on experience in event management, fashion promotion, and production logistics while applying industry-standard practices to showcase student designs in a professional setting.
May be repeated for credit.

title: Technology Applications for the Apparel Industry
description:
Introduction to the basic digital techniques of Adobe Creative Suite to develop presentation skills necessary in textile and apparel industries.
Prereq: ARMD 181 .

title: History of Fashion
description: Historic view of the evolution of fashion in the Western world through time as it relates to political/sociological/economic change. F.

title: Apparel Design Studio
description:
This course examines the principles of apparel construction, fit, and pattern design, guiding students through the process of creating well-constructed garments. Students will develop a cohesive clothing line by applying advanced apparel assembly techniques, constructing fitting muslins, and producing two fashion garments that reflect industry standards in design and craftsmanship.
May be repeated for credit.
Prereq: ARMD 101 or instructor approval via proficiency test.

title: Textiles
description: Fibers, yarns, fabric construction, finishes, and dyestuffs related to selection, use, and maintenance of textile products. Coreq: ARMD 367 .

title: Textiles Laboratory
description:
Textile product characterization through the analysis of yarn type, fabric, construction, finishes, and dyestuffs; care procedures; simple identification of fibers, yarns, and fabrics.
Coreq: ARMD 366 .

title: Sewn-Product Manufacturing and Analysis
description:
Analysis of the sewn-product manufacturing processes, governmental regulations, sourcing, and technology applications. Focus on evaluating products, quality, performance, and cost.
Prereq: ARMD 366 , ARMD 367 or departmental approval.

title: Fashion Trend Analysis and Forecasting
description:
Study of techniques and processes of identifying past and present trends and methods for forecasting future trends, with applications in the apparel and home fashion, textiles and retail industries.
Prereq: ARMD 181 and ARMD 280 .

title: Global Retailing
description:
Theoretical approach to management practices and marketing policies for retail soft goods in a complex and changing world market. ARMD 372 is restricted to Apparel, Retail Merchandising and Design or Hospitality and Event Management major or minor. MRKT 372 is restricted to College of Business major or minor and a 2.50 minimum NDSU grade point average.
Prereq: MRKT 320 or ARMD 171 .
Cross-listed with MRKT 372 .

title: Visual Merchandising and Promotion
description:
Principles, procedures and sources of information essential for marketing and promoting retail merchandise sales. Experience in planning, executing and evaluating promotion plans.
Prereq: ARMD 280 .

title: Professional Development
description:
Internship and career planning including professional expectations and responsibilities.  Skills gained in resume and portfolio development, writing cover letters, interviewing techniques, and business etiquette.
Prereq or Coreq: At least junior standing.

title: Global Fashion Economics
description:
Study of factors affecting production, distribution, and consumption of products in domestic and foreign textile and apparel industries.
Prereq: ARMD 171 and ECON 105 , ECON 201 , or ECON 202 .
Prereq or Coreq: Junior standing.

title: Merchandise Planning and Buying
description:
This course intends to provide an overview of merchandise planning and buying. This course will introduce students to the principles, mathematical formulas and concepts of retail buying and assortment planning.
Prereq: ARMD 171 .

title: Experiential Retailing
description:
Explore experiential retailing of products, services and experiences that encompass utilitarian and hedonic consumption.  Apply strategies for planning, developing, and presenting products or services to create a total consumer experience.
Prereq: ARMD 140 or ARMD 171 or MGMT 320 or MRKT 320 .
Dual-listing: ARMD 625 .

title: Apparel Design and Assembly
description: Application of the principles and concepts of pattern design and apparel assembly to the creation of garments in a particular category of apparel: tailored jackets, dresses, formalwear and casual sportswear. May be repeated. Prereq: ADHM 155.

title: Retail Financial Management and Control
description:
Advanced applications of the principles and mathematics in merchandise planning, buying, and control process.
Prereq: ARMD 386 , CSCI 114 or TL 116, MRKT 320 , MGMT 320 and ACCT 102 .

title: Product Development
description:
Examination of issues and strategies necessary to produce soft goods in relation to product development, sourcing and manufacturing. Focus on developing technical specifications relative to quality, performance and cost of apparel products.
Prereq: ADHM 280, ADHM 366, ADHM 367, ADHM 371 and ADHM 385.

title: Capstone in Apparel, Retail Merchandising and Design
description:
Critically analyze and propose research-based solutions to problems related to apparel and textiles including production, distribution, and retailing of goods and services.
Prereq: ARMD 385 and ENGL 320 or ENGL 322 or ENGL 326 or ENGL 357 .
Prereq or Coreq: Junior standing.

title: Dress and Human Behavior
description: Influence of dress and appearance on human behavior throughout the life cycle. F.

title: Study Tour
description:
Faculty-directed tour to key fashion, design, tourism destinations, or business centers in the U.S. and abroad. Visits to off-campus destinations provide students contact with practicing professionals as they are exposed to the fast pace of a changing global industry.
May be repeated.
Prereq: ARMD 140 or ARMD 160, ARMD 171 .

title: Experiential Retailing
description:
Explore experiential retailing of products, services and experiences that encompass utilitarian and hedonic consumption. Apply strategies for planning, developing, and presenting products or services to create a total consumer experience.
Dual-listing: ARMD 425 .

title: Consumer Behavior in Merchandising
description: Evaluation of psychological, sociological, and cultural theories of consumer behavior through the examination of factors influencing the consumer decision-making process.

title: Professional Advancement
description: Analysis of leadership and how it affects organizational culture and change through past and current experiences. Various leadership styles examined and a personal leadership philosophy developed for professional advancement in merchandising.

title: Product Design, Development and Evaluation
description: Advanced study of issues and management strategies necessary to design and produce a competitively priced product. Examination of the role of globalization and rapidly changing technology on the development of a successful product.

title: Entrepreneurship in Dietetics
description: The economics of entrepreneurship, business plan development, and steps in starting your own business related to hospitality or dietetics, including consultation.

title: Promotional Strategies in Merchandising
description: Examination of integrated marketing communications (i.e., promotional strategies and techniques) while fostering cultural and global awareness, social responsibility and ethical decision-making in the field of promotion.

title: Retail Theory and Current Practice
description: Theoretical and applied analysis of merchandising strategies; assessment of internal and external environmental forces impacting strategic decisions by retail firms; synthesis of past and present trends in order to forecast probable future patterns.

title: Merchandising Analytics
description: Merchandising Analytics addresses the use of quantitative data from the merchandising industry to support managerial decision making: specifically, how to format and analyze typical consumer data. Students will apply analytical approaches to problem solving using Microsoft Excel including: Formulas, Functions, Solver, and Pivot Tables.

title: International Retail Expansion
description:
Comprehensive understanding of theory, practices, and trends on international merchandise management. An analysis of global retail system and the way goods are distributed to consumers in various countries.
Prereq: ARMD 710 , ARMD 720 , ARMD 730 , ARMD 740 , ARMD 750 .

title: Research Methods in Merchandising
description:
An overview of the research process used in social science, including an overview and analysis of research methodologies. Also includes a review of current merchandising literature with implications for future research.
Prereq: ARMD 710 , ARMD 720 , ARMD 730 , ARMD 740 , ARMD 750 .
Prereq or Coreq: Graduate level statistics course.

title: Financial Merchandising Implications
description:
The advanced study of financial trends in the merchandising industries; implications related to varied organizational structures. Foci will be on the financial implications of recent advances in the field.
Prereq: ARMD 710 , ARMD 720 , ARMD 730 , ARMD 740 , ARMD 750 .

title: Strategic Merchandise Planning
description:
Examination of the executive planning process utilized to develop successful corporate strategies; emphasis on the importance of a market orientation for building customer value and sustaining a competitive advantage.
Prereq: ARMD 710 , ARMD 720 , ARMD 730 , ARMD 740 , ARMD 750 .

title: Creativity and Communication
description:
Introductory course that covers the key aspects of the creative problem-solving process as applied in the design of buildings, the landscape, and communities, with an introduction to design visualization and visual thinking, information management, design methods, design process management, studio culture, and professional communication.
Prereq or Coreq: Architecture majors only.

title: Design Technology
description: Introductory exploration of digital design media and environmental technology in architecture and landscape architecture. Prereq or Co-req: ARCH 271 or LA 271 .

title: Architectural Design I
description:
Studio course focused on beginning exercises in basic design, incorporating abstract two-dimensional design, functional response to environmental determinants, the articulation of form, spatial organization, and aesthetic judgment.
Prereq or Coreq: Architecture majors only.

title: Architectural Design II
description: Studio course focused on exercises in basic design incorporating abstract two-dimensional design, functional response to environmental determinants, the articulation of form, spatial organization, and aesthetic judgment. Prereq: ARCH 271 .

title: Seminar
description: A group of students engaged, under a professor or professors, in research or criticism and in presentation of reports pertaining thereto.

title: Global Practicum: Study Abroad
description: Pre-Arranged study at accredited foreign institutions (study abroad), domestic institutions (National Student Exchange) or on approved study abroad programs. Pre-requisite: Sophomore standing and prior approval by International Student and Study Abroad Services and major department. Graded 'P'or 'F' (Undergraduate), or 'S' or 'U' (Graduate).

title: History and Theory of Architecture I
description: History and theory of architecture from ancient times through the Renaissance with attention placed on the design connections across cultures and across the globe. Lecture course.

title: History and Theory of Architecture II
description: History of architecture from the Baroque to the mid-range of modernism, placed within a global perspective. Lecture course.

title: History and Theory of Architecture III
description: History and theory of architecture from the mid-range of modernism to the contemporary, exploring different movements, methods, and ideas, including global and vernacular influences. Prereq: Admission into the second year of the Architecture or Landscape Architecture program.

title: Site Design for Architects
description: Intermediate investigations and applied learning activities directed towards understanding building site inventory, analysis, and appropriate design responses.  Prereq: ARCH 272 .

title: Architectural Structures I
description: Overview of the principles of statics and mechanics of materials and structural concepts relative to building members and frames. Prereq: ARCH 271 and admission to the Architecture program.

title: Materials & Construction
description: Study of building materials from source through manufacture, focusing on their contribution to design and the study of the assembly processes of construction. Lecture course. Prereq: ARCH 272 .

title: Architectural Design III
description: Studio courses providing intermediate level exercises in architectural design; responding to contextual, cultural, environmental, climatic, technological, and aesthetic determinants. Prereq: ARCH 272 .

title: Architectural Design IV
description: Studio course continuing intermediate level exercises in architectural design: responding to contextual, cultural, environmental, climatic, technological, and aesthetic determinants. Prereq: ARCH 371 .

title: Faculty-Led Study Abroad
description: NDSU instructed experience or field study in a foreign country. Conducted in English for NDSU resident credit. Requires Global Programs and Services, and departmental approval. May be repeated for credit.

title: Global Practicum: Study Abroad
description: Pre-Arranged study at accredited foreign institutions (study abroad), domestic institutions (National Student Exchange) or on approved study abroad programs. Pre-requisite: Sophomore standing and prior approval by International Student and Study Abroad Services and major department. Graded 'P'or 'F' (Undergraduate), or 'S' or 'U' (Graduate).

title: Architectural Structures II
description: Overview of the principles of statics and mechanics of materials and structural concepts relative to building members and frames. Prereq: ARCH 344 , ARCH 372 .

title: Advanced Systems and Environment
description:
Overview of the building systems integration approach to the architectural design process.
Prereq: ARCH 344 , ARCH 471 or ARCH 474 .
Dual-listing: ARCH 644 .

title: Architectural Detailing
description:
Study of wood, steel, masonry, concrete, and other miscellaneous construction assemblies, focusing on the detail, with an introduction to specifications and construction documents.
Prereq: ARCH 371 .

title: Environmental Control Systems: Passive Principles
description:
Study of architectural design related to thermal comfort, climate, passive solar systems, daylighting, and other environmental concerns.
Prereq: ARCH 272 .
Dual-listing: ARCH 653 .

title: Environmental Control System: Active System
description: Study of the basic fundamentals of illumination and basic power generation, distribution and service; heating, ventilation, and air-conditioning systems; plumbing systems; and acoustics as they relate to building design. Prereq: ARCH 371 , 453 .

title: Urban Design
description: Study of urban form and urban theory, development, and processes in a historic and contemporary context. Prereq: ARCH 371 or LA 371.

title: Architectural Design V
description: Studio courses involving the complex organization of architectural spaces and forms in an urban context. Prereq: ARCH 372 .

title: Architectural Design VI
description: Studio courses involving the complex organization of architectural spaces and forms in an urban context. Prereq: ARCH 471 .

title: International Design Studio
description: Comprehensive design studio experience in advanced architectural studies to be conducted in culturally diverse, international locations. Prereq: ARCH 471 .

title: Global Practicum: Study Abroad
description: Pre-Arranged study at accredited foreign institutions (study abroad), domestic institutions (National Student Exchange) or on approved study abroad programs. Pre-requisite: Sophomore standing and prior approval by International Student and Study Abroad Services and major department. Graded 'P'or 'F' (Undergraduate), or 'S' or 'U' (Graduate).

title: Architectural Structures II
description:
Overview of the principles of statics and mechanics of materials and structural concepts relative to building members and frames
Prereq or Coreq: Admission to Department of Architecture graduate MArch program.
Dual-listing: ARCH 443 .

title: Advanced Systems and Environment
description:
Overview of the building systems integration approach to the architectural design process.
Dual-listing: ARCH 444 .

title: Architectural Detailing
description:
Study of wood, steel, masonry, concrete, and other miscellaneous construction assemblies, focusing on the detail, with an introduction to specifications and construction documents.
Dual-listing: ARCH 450 .

title: Environmental Control Systems - Passive Principles
description:
Study of architectural design related to thermal comfort, climate, passive solar systems, daylighting, acoustics, and other environmental concerns.
Dual-listing: ARCH 453 .

title: Urban Design
description:
Study of urban form and urban theory, development, and processes in a historic and contemporary context.
Dual-listing: ARCH 461 .

title: Architectural Design V
description:
Studio courses involving the complex organization of architectural spaces and forms in an urban context.
Prereq or Coreq: Admission to the NDSU Graduate MArch degree program.
Dual-listing: ARCH 471 .

title: Architectural Design VI
description:
Studio courses involving the complex organization of architectural spaces and forms in an urban context.
Prereq or Coreq: Admission to the NDSU Graduate MArch degree program.
Dual-listing: ARCH 472 .

title: Non-Western Architectural Traditions
description: Advanced course on the investigation of design methods and building traditions of non-Western cultures and diverse geographic regions.  May be repeated.

title: Urbanism
description: Advanced course to explore in-depth aspects of urban design.  May be repeated.

title: Historic Preservation
description: Advanced course to explore the philosophy and techniques of preserving historic buildings.  May be repeated.

title: Architectural Technology
description: Advanced course to explore the historical and theoretical underpinnings of architectural technology.  May be repeated.

title: Architecture or the Recent Past
description: Advanced course to explore the major architectural movements and personalities since the mid-20th century.  May be repeated.

title: Current Architectural Theory
description: Advanced course focused on current issues and the work and design theory of leading architectural practitioners around the world.  May be repeated.

title: Vernacular Architectural Traditions
description: Advanced course to explore vernacular architectural traditions in North America and elsewhere.  May be repeated.

title: Sociocultural Issues
description: Advanced course focused on the social issues and movements that have influenced environmental design. May be repeated.

title: Thesis Research
description: An introduction to research methodologies and the research required to complete a design thesis in a first professional degree program in Architecture at the graduate level. Completion of a Thesis Proposal and a Thesis Research document in preparation for the Design Thesis Studio to follow. Prereq: ARCH 472 .

title: Advanced Architectural Design
description: Advanced studio course addressing complex design problems requiring increased self-direction.

title: Design Thesis
description:
Advanced studio course devoted to the execution of a comprehensive design thesis project, from schematic design through design development, presentation, and review.
Prereq: ARCH 763 and ARCH 771 .

title: Professional Practice
description: Study of contemporary architectural practice covering professional development, firm organization, and project management within the context of the ethical, legal, and regulatory environment.  Cross-listed with LA 581 .

title: Professional Topics in Architecture
description: Various topics related to theorectical or methodological aspects of architecture as a professional discipline.  May be repeated.

title: Seminar
description: A group of students engaged, under a professor or professors, in research or criticism and in presentation of reports pertaining thereto.

title: Introduction to the Visual Arts
description: Study and analysis of artistic methods and meaning in the visual arts; designed for non-majors.

title: Introduction to Art History
description: Survey of world art from prehistoric to modern times designed for non-majors.

title: Painting I
description: Introduction to basic painting through a variety of materials. Includes historical examples, painting the human figure, using acrylics, oils, pastel, and mixed-media.

title: Studio Technology Foundations
description:
Studio Technology Foundations is an introductory-level course in the Department of Art & Design. Lectures, demonstrations, and class assignments offer students an introduction to core digital technologies in the visual arts and equip students with a working knowledge of their respective operations and applications.
Prereq or Coreq: Graphic Design major or minor, Art major or minor, Art Education major, or Apparel Retail Merchandising major or minor.

title: Foundations of Design
description: Foundations of Design is an introductory course that provides a comprehensive study of design elements and principles for work in two and three-dimensions.

title: Drawing I
description: Study and application of different drawing media, methods, and techniques. Drawing from the human figure required.

title: Foundations Drawing
description: This course emphasizes observational and technical skill development in a variety of media to solve literal and conceptual problems relating to the history of drawing.

title: Ceramics I
description: Introduction to basic ceramic techniques. Includes wheel-throwing and hand-building techniques, surface decoration, glazing, and firing.

title: Design Thinking and Creative Strategy
description: This course offers an introduction to the theory of art and design production, design processes, and critical theory, as it relates to the creation and reception of visual arts. Through a combination of lectures, reading discussions, and in-class activities students will learn strategies to solve problems and effectively move ideas into visual/conceptual statements.

title: Sculpture I
description: Introduction to basic sculpture materials and techniques. Includes exploration of sculptural form in maquettes and large-scale work; additive and subtractive approaches in wood, stone, and mixed media; casting practice in plaster and hydro-stone.

title: Printmaking I
description: Introduction to basic printmaking techniques and materials. Includes mono-print, collagraph, intaglio, relief, and serigraphy in both traditional and nontoxic methods.

title: Photography I
description: Introduction to basic photography. Includes visual issues of black and white and color photography. Experience with black and white processing and printing.

title: Graphic Design I
description:
Introduction to the foundations and principles of graphic design and the terms, tools, and concepts used in the study and creation of contemporary, graphic design solutions.
Prereq: ART 122 .
Prereq or Coreq: Must be declared a Graphic Design major or minor, Art major or minor, Art Education major, or Apparel Retail Merchandising major or minor.

title: Art History I
description: Intensive survey of art from Paleolithic to the Renaissance.

title: Art History II
description: Intensive survey of art from the Renaissance to the present.

title: Painting II
description: Intermediate study, studio practice, and critique. Use of oils, acrylics, watercolor, and mixed media. Painting the human figure and development of individual concept and content. Prereq: ART 120 .

title: Drawing II
description: Advanced study and application of different drawing media, methods, techniques and drawing the human figure. Prereq: ART 130 .

title: Ceramics II
description: Intermediate study, studio practice, and critique. Development of individual concept and content. Further exploration of forming skills and surface decoration. Introduction to basic mold techniques, clay and glaze theory, and kiln technology. Prereq: ART 150 .

title: Sculpture II
description: Intermediate study, practice, and critique. Further exploration of materials and processes.  Development of individual concept and content. Prereq: ART 160 .

title: Printmaking II
description: Intermediate study, studio practice, and critique. Extension of process and media. Development of individual concept and content. Prereq: ART 170 .

title: Digital Image and Output
description: Introduction to digital photography workflow, digital image editing software, and inkjet printing. Prereq: ART 180 .

title: Seminar
description: A group of students engaged, under a professor or professors, in research or criticism and in presentation of reports pertaining thereto.

title: Global Practicum: Study Abroad
description: Pre-Arranged study at accredited foreign institutions (study abroad), domestic institutions (National Student Exchange) or on approved study abroad programs. Pre-requisite: Sophomore standing and prior approval by International Student and Study Abroad Services and major department. Graded 'P'or 'F' (Undergraduate), or 'S' or 'U' (Graduate).

title: Topics in Painting
description: Instruction in topics related to the advanced study of painting. Studio techniques, project development, and effective visual and oral communication practices are emphasized. May be repeated for credit. Prereq: ART 120 .

title: Drawing III
description: Advanced study, studio practice, and critique. Use of mixed-media approaches. Emphasis on individual concept and content. Life drawing emphasis. Prereq: ART 230 .

title: Figure Drawing
description: Exploration of the human form through drawing representationally, abstractly and expressively using a variety of media. Studying historic and contemporary use of the figure will be significant. Prereq: ART 130 .

title: Topics in Ceramics
description: Instruction in topics related to the advanced study of ceramics. Studio techniques, project development, and effective visual and oral communication practices are emphasized. May be repeated for credit. Prereq: ART 150 .

title: Sculpture III
description: Advanced study, practice, and critique. Use of mixed-media. Specialization in materials and processes. Emphasis on individual concept and content. Prereq: ART 260 .

title: Topics in Printmaking
description: Instruction in topics related to the advanced study of printmaking. Studio techniques, project development, and effective visual and oral communication practices are emphasized. May be repeated for credit. Prereq: ART 170 .

title: Faculty-Led Study Abroad
description: NDSU instructed experience or field study in a foreign country. Conducted in English for NDSU resident credit. Requires Global Programs and Services, and departmental approval. May be repeated for credit.

title: Topics in Photography
description: Instruction in topics related to the advanced study of photography. Studio techniques, project development, and effective visual and oral communication practices are emphasized. May be repeated for credit. Prereq: ART 180 .

title: Typography & Layout
description:
Development of concepts and practices related to graphic design, digital technologies and their applications in design and visual arts, with an emphasis on the study and application of typography in design work and layout design.
Prereq: ART 185 .
Prereq or Coreq: Must be declared a Art major or minor, Art Education major, Apparel Retail Merchandising Design major or minor, or Graphic Design major or minor.

title: Branding & Packaging
description:
Development of concepts and practices related to graphic design, digital technologies and their applications in design and visual arts, with an emphasis on developing branding and packaging for design opportunities.
Prereq: ART 185 .
Prereq or Coreq: Must be declared as a Graphic Design major or minor, Art major or minor, Art Education major, or Apparel Retail Merchandising Design major or minor.

title: Illustration Concepts & Narrative
description:
Advanced study of concepts and studio practice related to digital technologies and their applications in graphic design and visual arts with an emphasis on illustration and visual narratives.
Prereq: ART 185 .
Prereq or Coreq: Must be declared Graphic Design major or minor, Art major or minor, Art Education major, or Apparel Retail Merchandising Design major or minor.

title: Motion Graphics & Animation
description:
Advanced study of concepts and studio practice related to digital technologies and their applications in graphic design and visual arts, with an emphasis in motion graphics and animation.
Prereq: ART 185 .
Prereq or Coreq: Must be declared Graphic Design major or minor, Art major or minor, Art Education major, or Apparel Retail Merchandising Design major or minor.

title: Advanced Topics in Graphic Design
description: Development of concepts and practices related to graphic design, digital technologies and their applications in design and visual arts. May be repeated for credit. Prereq: ART 185 .

title: Art Theory and Criticism
description: This course covers the development and application of art theory and criticism from the advent of photography to the present.  The course presents theory and criticism as fluid methods of understanding art.  The course covers standard theories as well as their critical applications. Prereq: ART 210 , ART 211 .

title: Advanced Studio Arts Development
description: Instruction in topics related to the advanced study and development of the studio arts. Studio techniques, project iteration, and effective visual and oral communication practices are emphasized. May be repeated for credit. Corequisite: ART 391 or ART 491 . Prerequisite: Requires one from the following: ART 120 , ART 150 , ART 160 , ART 170 , ART 180 or ART 185 .

title: Global Practicum: Study Abroad
description: Pre-Arranged study at accredited foreign institutions (study abroad), domestic institutions (National Student Exchange) or on approved study abroad programs. Pre-requisite: Sophomore standing and prior approval by International Student and Study Abroad Services and major department. Graded 'P'or 'F' (Undergraduate), or 'S' or 'U' (Graduate).

title: Baccalaureate Studio: Painting
description: This course facilitates studio production for the Baccalaureate process. Work-flow will follow specific deadlines that accompany the critique schedule in ART 489 . You will be working with your studio professor to prepare and develop a body of work that will culminate in an exhibition at the end of the semester. May be repeated for credit. Prereq: ART 320 .

title: Drawing IV
description: Advanced study, studio practice and critique in drawing. Exploration in individual concept, process and professional preparation. May be repeated. Prereq: ART 330 .

title: Advanced Figure Drawing
description: Advanced study, studio practice and critique in figure drawing. Continued exploration of the human form and development of an individual concept. May be repeated. Prereq: ART 335 .

title: Baccalaureate Studio: Ceramics
description: This course facilitates studio production for the Baccalaureate process. Work-flow will follow specific deadlines that accompany the critique schedule in ART 489 . You will be working with your studio professor to prepare and develop a body of work that will culminate in an exhibition at the end of the semester. May be repeated for credit. Prereq: ART 350 .

title: History of American Art
description: Study of American art from pre-Columbian through contemporary (including Native American), emphasizing its highly individual nature and its effect on world art. Prereq: ART 210 , ART 211 .

title: Contemporary Art
description: Study of the development of contemporary art examining its cultural and intellectual basis; includes analysis of current art imagery and readings in art theory and criticism.

title: Topics in Art History
description: As an upper-division course in a specialized topic in Art History, the subject matter of the course varies by semester, allowing the curriculum to be more responsive and flexible in the subjects it addresses. May be repeated. Prereq: ART 210 or ART 211 .

title: Sculpture IV
description: Advanced study, studio practice and critique in sculpture. A focus on current issues in sculpture with innovative use of form, process and materials centered in a personal use of content and formal issues. May be repeated. Prereq: ART 360 .

title: Baccalaureate Studio: Printmaking
description: This course facilitates studio production for the Baccalaureate process. Work-flow will follow specific deadlines that accompany the critique schedule in ART 489 . You will be working with your studio professor to prepare and develop a body of work that will culminate in an exhibition at the end of the semester. May be repeated for credit. Prereq: ART 370 .

title: Baccalaureate Studio: Photography
description: This course facilitates studio production for the Baccalaureate process. Work-flow will follow specific deadlines that accompany the critique schedule in ART 489 . You will be working with your studio professor to prepare and develop a body of work that will culminate in an exhibition at the end of the semester. May be repeated for credit. Prereq: ART 380 .

title: Baccalaureate Studio: Graphic Design
description: This course facilitates studio production for the Baccalaureate process. Work-flow will follow specific deadlines that accompany the critique schedule in ART 489 . You will be working with your studio professor to prepare and develop a body of work that will culminate in an exhibition at the end of the semester. May be repeated for credit. Prereq: ART 385 .

title: Baccalaureate Project
description: Capstone research and creative experience within a specific area of interest with emphasis on refinement of aesthetic applications of techniques and media.  May be repeated.

title: Global Practicum: Study Abroad
description: Pre-Arranged study at accredited foreign institutions (study abroad), domestic institutions (National Student Exchange) or on approved study abroad programs. Pre-requisite: Sophomore standing and prior approval by International Student and Study Abroad Services and major department. Graded 'P'or 'F' (Undergraduate), or 'S' or 'U' (Graduate).

title: Intercollegiate Sports Participation
description: Participation on an intercollegiate sports team.  May be repeated.

title: Intercollegiate Sports Participation
description: Participation on an intercollegiate sports team.  May be repeated.

title: Non-Majors Biology Lab
description: Laboratory experience to introduce the application of the scientific method across a wide scale of biological topics, including molecular biology, the organism, evolution, and ecology. This lab may be taken as a co-requisite with BIOL 111 , BIOL 124 or BIOL 126 .

title: Human Anatomy and Physiology Prep
description: This course is designed to improve the success of students when taking Human Anatomy and Physiology. The focus of the course will be to develop language literacy, visual literacy, study skills, and the understanding of science concepts that are fundamental to Human Anatomy and Physiology. Restrictions: First-year students.

title: Concepts of Biology
description: Introduction to a wide range of biological topics, from the organism, ecology, and evolution to the cell, molecular biology, and genetics. For credit as a lecture/lab pair, students should take BIOL 100L as a co-requisite.

title: Environmental Science
description: Ecological principles related to human cultures, resource use, and environmental alterations. For credit as a lecture/lab pair, students should take BIOL 100L as a co-requisite.

title: Human Biology
description: Consideration of selected problems in human biology. For credit as a lecture/lab pair, students should take BIOL 100L as a co-requisite.

title: General Biology I
description: Introduction to cellular and molecular biology, genetics, and evolution.

title: General Biology I Laboratory
description: Introduction to cellular and molecular biology, genetics, and evolution.

title: General Biology II
description: An introduction to the biology of living organisms and their interactions with each other and their environments. Examples primarily involve plants and animals, but include other groups of organisms as well.

title: General Biology II Laboratory
description: An introduction to the biology of living organisms and their interactions with each other and their environments. Examples primarily involve plants and animals, but include other groups of organisms as well.

title: Skills for Academic Success
description: This course is designed to ease the transition for new students at NDSU. Students will be introduced to skills and techniques used by successful college students. Topics will include campus resources, study techniques, time management, goal setting, degree planning, and career and major orientation.

title: Seminar
description: A group of students engaged, under a professor or professors, in research or criticism and in presentation of reports pertaining thereto.

title: Human Anatomy and Physiology I
description: An in-depth introduction to structure and function of human organ systems' cells, tissues, the integumentary system, the skeletal system, joints, muscle and muscular system, nervous tissue and nervous system, and the special senses. F.

title: Human Anatomy and Physiology I Laboratory
description: An in-depth introduction to structure and function of human organ systems' cells, tissues, the integumentary system, the skeletal system, joints, muscle and muscular system, nervous tissue and nervous system, and the special senses. F.

title: Human Anatomy and Physiology II
description: A continuation of BIOL 220 , 220L ; the endocrine, cardiovascular, lymphatic, immune, respiratory, digestive, urinary, and reproductive systems and development. Prereq: BIOL 220 . S.

title: Human Anatomy and Physiology II Laboratory
description: A continuation of BIOL 220 , 220L ; the endocrine, cardiovascular, lymphatic, immune, respiratory, digestive, urinary, and reproductive systems and development. S.

title: Undergraduate Research Experience: Antibiotic Discovery
description: This course is an authentic research experience for undergraduates. Students in the course will participate in a larger national research initiative aimed at discovering new antibiotics produced by soil bacteria. Prereq: BIOL 150 and BIOL 151 .

title: Undergraduate Research Experience: Field Biology and Experimental Ecology
description:
This course is an authentic research experience for undergraduates. Students in the course will participate in collaborative research projects with their teams, each team will design its own unique project focused on the wildlife population of interest.
Prereq: BIOL 151L .

title: Undergraduate Research Experience: Health and Wellness Decisions
description:
This course is an authentic research experience for undergraduates. Students in the course will explore the processes of learning and instruction in undergraduate biology through critical reading of literature, experimental design, data analysis, and communication of research findings.
Prereq: BIOL 151L .

title: Undergraduate Research Experience: Genomic Analysis
description: This course is an authentic research experience that provides students the opportunity to learn lab and analysis techniques used in genomics research, including sequencing of prokaryotic and eukaroytic DNA, and annotation of Drosophila sp. DNA. Prereq: BIOL 150 and BIOL 150L and BIOL 151 and BIOL 151L .

title: Undergraduate Research Experience: Insect Behavior
description: This course is an authentic research experience for undergraduates focused on learning the methods used to study animal behavior. Students in the course will participate in collaborative research projects with their teams - each team will design its own unique project focused on the behavior of aphids and their predators/parasitoids. Prereq: BIOL 150 , BIOL 150L , BIOL 151 , BIOL 151L .

title: Seminar
description: A group of students engaged, under a professor or professors, in research or criticism and in presentation of reports pertaining thereto.

title: Global Practicum: Study Abroad
description: Pre-Arranged study at accredited foreign institutions (study abroad), domestic institutions (National Student Exchange) or on approved study abroad programs. Pre-requisite: Sophomore standing and prior approval by International Student and Study Abroad Services and major department. Graded 'P'or 'F' (Undergraduate), or 'S' or 'U' (Graduate).

title: Genetics
description: Study of the basis of heredity with emphasis on structure and function of DNA and Mendelian genetics. 3 lectures. Cross-listed with PLSC 315 . F, S.

title: Genetics Laboratory
description: Study of the basis of heredity with emphasis on structure and function of DNA and Mendelian genetics. 1 two-hour laboratory. Cross-listed with PLSC 315L . F, S.

title: Evolution
description: Evolution is the process by which species change over time through descent with modification. This course will focus on understanding the key biological concepts of heritability of traits, variation, adaptation through selection and evolutionary change at all scales. Prereq: BIOL 150 , BIOL 151 .

title: General Ecology
description: Ecological principles associated with organism environment interactions, populations, communities, and ecosystems. Quantitative approach with examples (animal, plant, microbial) included. Prereq: BIOL 150 and BIOL 151 .

title: Cell Biology
description: Structure and function of cells, including cell surfaces, membranes, organelles, cytoskeleton, cell division, cell physiology, and methods used in cell studies. Prereq: BIOL 150 and BIOL 151 .

title: Faculty-Led Study Abroad
description: NDSU instructed experience or field study in a foreign country. Conducted in English for NDSU resident credit. Requires Global Programs and Services, and departmental approval. May be repeated for credit.

title: Global Practicum: Study Abroad
description: Pre-Arranged study at accredited foreign institutions (study abroad), domestic institutions (National Student Exchange) or on approved study abroad programs. Pre-requisite: Sophomore standing and prior approval by International Student and Study Abroad Services and major department. Graded 'P'or 'F' (Undergraduate), or 'S' or 'U' (Graduate).

title: Science Communication
description:
Introduction to the principles of communicating scientific issues to the general public and practical application of communication strategies through interpersonal discussion, media, and other means.
Cross-listed with COMM 401 .
Dual-listing: BIOL 601 , COMM 601 .

title: Comparative Chordate Morphology
description: This is a combination lecture/lab course designed to introduce you to the systematics, history, and structure of the chordates, particularly the craniates.  Structural and functional similarities and differences among organs and organ systems of representative organisms as well as the use of comparative morphology as a tool to better understand the process of chordate evolution will be emphasized. Prereq: BIOL 150 and BIOL 151 .

title: Plant Taxonomy
description:
An introduction to the identification, classification and evolution of vascular plants, with an emphasis on flowering plants (the angiosperms). Students will learn techniques for plant identification and the characteristic traits of plant families worldwide within the framework of our current understanding of plant evolutionary relationships. Students will use weekly field trips to develop their skills in collection and identification of plants.
Prereq: BIOL 151 or by permission of instructor.
Dual-listing: BIOL 614 .

title: Vertebrate Histology
description: Study of the microscopic anatomy of vertebrate tissues and organs, especially mammals. Classification and identification of epithelium, connective, muscle, and nervous tissue types. Study of these tissues types in the context of major organ systems (circulatory, lymphoid, endocrine, integumentary, digestive, urinary, and respiratory). Prereq: BIOL 150 , BIOL 151 .

title: Invertebrate Zoology
description: Survey of the biology, classification, and evolution of invertebrates. Emphasis on major phyla, marine, and parasitic taxa. Prereq: BIOL 150 , BIOL 151 . {Also offered for graduate credit - see BIOL 650 .}.

title: Ichthyology
description: Biology and taxonomy of fishes. Prereq: BIOL 151 . {Also offered for graduate credit - see BIOL 652 .}.

title: Herpetology
description: Primarily a field and laboratory course focusing on amphibians and reptiles. Students will learn about the biology, ecology, evolution, and life history of reptiles and amphibians utilizing a hands-on approach. Prereq: BIOL 150 , BIOL 151 . {Also offered for graduate credit - see BIOL 654 .}.

title: Ornithology
description: Introduction to the biology, classification, and identification of birds, especially local forms. Early morning field trips required. Prereq: BIOL 150 , BIOL 151 . {Also offered for graduate credit - see BIOL 656 .}.

title: Mammalogy
description: Biology and taxonomy of mammals. Prereq: BIOL 150 , BIOL 151 . F {Also offered for graduate credit - see BIOL 658 .}.

title: Animal Physiology
description: Study of the physical and chemical principles that govern cell, tissue,  organ, organ system, and organismal function. Prereq: BIOL 150 , BIOL 151 . {Also offered for graduate credit - see BIOL 660 .}.

title: Plant Ecology
description: Ecological structure, processes, and patterns observed with plant communities and populations as influenced by environmental conditions. Illustrations provided with local fieldwork. Prereq: BIOL 150 , BIOL 151 .{Also offered for graduate credit - see BIOL 661 .}.

title: Physiological Ecology
description: Study of the physiological mechanisms underlying life-history trade-offs and constraints in an ecological and evolutionary context. Prereq: BIOL 150 , BIOL 151 . {Also offered for graduate credit - see BIOL 662 .}.

title: Animal Behavior
description: Description of the principal behavior patterns of animals with consideration of ecological, evolutionary, and internal mechanisms. Prereq: BIOL 151 , BIOL 151L .

title: Endocrinology
description: Physiology and anatomy of endocrine glands; chemistry and interrelations of their secretions. Prereq: BIOL 150 , BIOL 151 , and BIOL 370 . {Also offered for graduate credit - see BIOL 664 .}.

title: Hormones and Behavior
description: Study of the organizational and activational role endocrine systems play in regulating animal behaviors. These studies will be explored within an ecological and evolutionary framework. Prereq: BIOL 150 and BIOL 151 . {Also offered for graduate credit - see BIOL 665 .}.

title: Freshwater Ecology and Limnology
description: Freshwater ecology is the study of the relationship between freshwater organisms and their environment. Limnology is the study of inland waters, including lakes, reservoirs, rivers, streams, wetlands and groundwater. This course will provide an overview of freshwater ecology & limnology, emphasizing fundamental interactions and processes. Prereq: BIOL 150 and BIOL 151 . {Also offered for graduate credit - See BIOL 670.}.

title: Structure and Diversity of Plants and Fungi
description: We will focus on structure and morphology of plants and fungi, as well as explore the diversity or adaptations plants and fungi have acquired to overcome a variety of environmental and habitat challenges. Prereq: BIOL 150 , BIOL 151 .

title: Organismal Form and Function: An Undergraduate Research Course
description:
This course is an authentic research experience for undergraduates. Students in the course will participate in collaborative research projects with their teams, each team will design its own unique project focused on the analysis of shape, size, biomechanics, or performance of an organism in regards to some evolutionary or experimental condition.
Prereq: BIOL 270 or BIOL 271 or BIOL 272 or BIOL 273 or BIOL 275 .

title: Undergraduate Research Experience: Biomedical Research Analysis
description:
This is an authentic course-based research experience for undergraduates. Through critical reading of the literature, students in this course will explore elements of biomedical research including the ethics of working with human subjects, experimental design of clinical studies, and statistical analysis and interpretation of results. The course will culminate with students  completing a meta-analysis to assess the effectiveness of a biomedical intervention of their choice.
Prereq: BIOL 150 , BIOL 150L , BIOL 151 and BIOL 151L .

title: Conservation Biology
description: Integrative approach to the study and conservation of biodiversity. Application of principles from various sub-disciplines of the biological and social sciences to current conservation problems. Prereq: BIOL 150 , BIOL 151 . {Also offered for graduate credit - see BIOL 675 .}.

title: Wildlife Ecology and Management
description: Application of ecological principles to management of game and non-game wildlife populations. Prereq: BIOL 150 and BIOL 151 . {Also offered for graduate credit - see BIOL 676 .}.

title: Wildlife and Fisheries Management Techniques
description: Students will learn techniques used in the study and management of fish and wildlife populations. Students will design an independent field research project to be executed during a field trip (typically 2-4 days in length). Prereq: BIOL 151 . {Also offered for graduate credit - see BIOL 677 .}.

title: Ecotoxicology
description: Ecotoxicology, the behavior of pollutants in and effects on ecosystems; top-down and bottom-up approaches for assessment/prediction of effects on populations, communities and ecosystems; ecotoxicological testing at single/multi-species levels; biomarkers; passive/active biomonitoring. Prereq: BIOL 151 and BIOL 151L . {Also offered for graduate credit - see BIOL 680 .}.

title: Wetland Science
description: Definition of wetlands, biogeochemistry, ecophysiology and adaptations to wetland conditions in plants and animals, biodiversity and productivity, wetland ecology, applications of wetlands for treatment of wastewater and other forms of bioremediation. Prereq: BIOL 151 , BIOL 151L . {Also offered for graduate credit - see BIOL 681 .}.

title: Developmental Biology
description: Analysis of the processes of development, with an emphasis on animal development. Topics range from classical embryology to the cellular and molecular basis of development. Prereq: BIOL 150 and BIOL 151 . {Also offered for graduate credit - see BIOL 682 .}.

title: Cellular Mechanisms of Diseases
description: This course will be focused on the cellular and molecular bases of selected diseases and some non-human animal diseases. Key cellular pathways/processes and molecular mechanisms that, when altered/disrupted, result in pathological changes/conditions will be discussed from scientific (e.g., functions, regulation and structures of cells, proteins and organs) and medical (e.g., clinical presentation, diagnostic and treatment) perspectives. Prereq: BIOL 150 , BIOL 151 , BIOL 315 or PLSC 315 , BIOL 370 . {Also offered for graduate credit - See BIOL 683 .}.

title: Cancer Biology
description:
This course is an introduction to the cellular and molecular basis of human cancers, with an emphasis on how malignant cells hijack normal cellular functions. The six major hallmarks of cancer will be the primary units of this course. Current issues, methodologies, and perspectives in modern cancer research will also be discussed.
Prereq: BIOL 370 with a grade of C or better and BIOL 150 .

title: Evolutionary Medicine and Genomics
description:
Evolution plays a key role in many areas of medicine, including vaccine design, antibiotic use, chemotherapy for cancer, drug discovery, and personalized medicine. This 3-credit lecture course explores the many ways that understanding human evolution, disease evolution, and cancer evolution gives us tools to be better health care workers and better patients.
Prereq: BIOL 150 , BIOL 151 and BIOL 315 or PLSC 315 .
Dual-listing: BIOL 685 .

title: Global Practicum: Study Abroad
description: Pre-Arranged study at accredited foreign institutions (study abroad), domestic institutions (National Student Exchange) or on approved study abroad programs. Pre-requisite: Sophomore standing and prior approval by International Student and Study Abroad Services and major department. Graded 'P'or 'F' (Undergraduate), or 'S' or 'U' (Graduate).

title: Science Communication
description:
Introduction to the principles of communicating scientific issues to the general public and practical application of communication strategies through interpersonal discussion, media, and other means.
Cross-listed with COMM 601 .
Dual-listing: BIOL 401 , COMM 401 .

title: Plant Taxonomy
description:
An introduction to the identification, classification and evolution of vascular plants, with an emphasis on flowering plants (the angiosperms). Students will learn techniques for plant identification and the characteristic traits of plant families worldwide within the framework of our current understanding of plant evolutionary relationships. Students will use weekly field trips to develop their skills in collection and identification of plants.
Dual-listing: BIOL 414 .

title: Invertebrate Zoology
description: Survey of the biology, classification, and evolution of invertebrates. Emphasis on major phyla, marine, and parasitic taxa. {Also offered for undergraduate credit - see BIOL 450 .}.

title: Ichthyology
description: Biology and taxonomy of fishes. {Also offered for undergraduate credit - see BIOL 452 .}.

title: Herpetology
description: Primarily a field and laboratory course focusing on amphibians and reptiles. Students will learn about the biology, ecology, evolution, and life history of reptiles and amphibians utilizing a hands-on approach. {Also offered for undergraduate credit - see BIOL 454 .}.

title: Ornithology
description: Introduction to the biology, classification, and identification of birds, especially local forms. Early morning field trips required. {Also offered for undergraduate credit - see BIOL 456 .}.

title: Mammalogy
description: Biology and taxonomy of mammals. {Also offered for undergraduate credit - see BIOL 458 .}.

description: Analysis of the processes of development, with an emphasis on animal development. Topics range from classical embryology to the cellular and molecular basis of development. {Also offered for undergraduate credit - see BIOL 482 .}.

See BIOL 483 .}.

title: Biomedical Genetics and Genomics
description: This course will cover the diagnoses, clinical presentations, prevention and treatments of hereditary diseases (Mendelian and complex); the ever-increasing roles that genetics and genomics have in advancing medicine (including personalized medicine). Dual-listing: BIOL 485 .

title: Global Practicum: Study Abroad
description: Pre-Arranged study at accredited foreign institutions (study abroad), domestic institutions (National Student Exchange) or on approved study abroad programs. Pre-requisite: Sophomore standing and prior approval by International Student and Study Abroad Services and major department. Graded 'P'or 'F' (Undergraduate), or 'S' or 'U' (Graduate).

title: Seminar
description: A group of students engaged, under a professor or professors, in research or criticism and in presentation of reports pertaining thereto.

title: Master's Paper
description: Literature review, research, and preparation for paper required for the comprehensive study option.  Graded S or U.

title: Advanced Cell Biology
description: In-depth survey of cell biology, including studies of membranes, secretion cytoskeleton, cellular movement organelles, and gene regulation.

title: Integrative Organismal Biology
description: This course will take an integrative approach in examining the evolutionary and ecological factors and the physiological mechanisms that contribute to variation among individuals in diverse organisms.

title: Quantitative Biology
description: Philosophy and techniques for collecting, handling, and interpreting research data in the biological sciences. S.

title: Advanced Conservation Biology
description: This class will cover recent developments in the field of conservation biology, with a specific focus on recent literature. Areas of focus will include Evolutionary Conservation and Conservation Genetics.

title: Evolution
description: Evolution is the process by which species change over time through descent with modification.  This course will focus on understanding the different applications of evolutionary theory to current issues in the biological sciences.

title: Evolutionary Ecology
description: Lecture-discussion course on recent developments in evolutionary theory and their implications in the study of animal adaptation, ecology, and behavior.

title: Advanced Physiology - Physiology of Extremes
description: This course will provide a greater understanding of the physiological systems of plants and animals. It exposes students to advanced physiological concepts and current literature perspectives on a variety of physiological systems and processes. This course is designed to increase the student's understanding of the mechanisms involved in the functioning of plant and animals with a specific focus on advanced topics and the physiology of extremes. A basic understanding of plant and animal physiology is expected prior to taking this course, as this course takes an in-depth look at the physiological extremes present in the plant and animal kingdoms.

title: Environment and Adaptation
description: Environmental factors and responses evidenced with life-history patterns, genetic variation, population dynamics, species-interactions, and physiological processes.

title: Ecological Processes
description: Ecosystem dynamics (short-term, successional, evolutionary), component interactions, ecological energetics, and biogeochemical transfers, with consideration of anthropogenic aspects. Historical and theoretical viewpoints included.

title: Biological Rhythms
description: This course will provide a greater understanding of the nature of endogenous time keeping ("clocks") and will investigate A) the biological mechanisms by which these 'clocks' interface with the environment, both biotic (e.g. social behavioral) and abiotic, to B) enable adaptive responses. This class will focus almost exclusively on vertebrates, but may include classic examples and insights gained from plants and invertebrates.

title: Advanced Animal Behavior
description: This course investigates current concepts and research areas in animal behavior, with a focus on topics that lie at the interface between animal behavior, ecology and evolution.

title: Population Dynamics
description: Principles and mechanics of animal population dynamics. Prereq: an interest in working with numbers.

title: Biological Research Principles
description: Discussion, analysis of published research papers, lectures on selected topics, and student research proposal. Prereq: STAT 725 .

title: Continuing Enrollment
description: Intended for graduate students who have completed all necessary credits of course work including thesis (798) and dissertation (899) on their approved Plan of Study, but who have not yet completed and submitted their thesis or dissertation. See the Enrollment Policy for details. The fee for Continuing Enrollment (898) is not eligible for a waiver. Students holding an F-1 or J-1 visa are not eligible to enroll in 898 credits as part of meeting requirements. If a student holding an F-1 or J-1 visa needs to enroll in an 898 course, they must receive prior approval to do so from the international student advisor in Global Programs and Services. Graded S/U. Requires department approval.

title: Elements of Biochemistry
description: Protein structure, function conformation, and dynamics; enzymes, DNA-RNA: structure and flow of genetic information; biological membranes; metabolism. 4 lectures. Prereq: CHEM 117 or CHEM 122 and CHEM 140 or CHEM 240 .

title: Seminar
description: A group of students engaged, under a professor or professors, in research or criticism and in presentation of reports pertaining thereto.

title: Global Practicum: Study Abroad
description: Pre-Arranged study at accredited foreign institutions (study abroad), domestic institutions (National Student Exchange) or on approved study abroad programs. Pre-requisite: Sophomore standing and prior approval by International Student and Study Abroad Services and major department. Graded 'P'or 'F' (Undergraduate), or 'S' or 'U' (Graduate).

title: The Science of Learning
description: This course is designed for students serving as Learning Assistants in the College of Science and Mathematics and who are interested in the science behind learning in the STEM disciplines.

title: Fundamentals of Forensic DNA Analysis
description: Principles, technologies, and analysis of genetic information (DNA) and its applications to forensic science, in particular, identity profiling.

title: Faculty-Led Study Abroad
description: NDSU instructed experience or field study in a foreign country. Conducted in English for NDSU resident credit. Requires Global Programs and Services, and departmental approval. May be repeated for credit.

title: Global Practicum: Study Abroad
description: Pre-Arranged study at accredited foreign institutions (study abroad), domestic institutions (National Student Exchange) or on approved study abroad programs. Pre-requisite: Sophomore standing and prior approval by International Student and Study Abroad Services and major department. Graded 'P'or 'F' (Undergraduate), or 'S' or 'U' (Graduate).

title: Foundations of Biochemistry and Molecular Biology I
description: Rigorous treatment of biomolecules, generation and use of metabolic energy, biosynthesis, metabolic regulation; storage, transmission, and expression of genetic information.  3 lectures. Prereq: CHEM 240 or CHEM 341 . Recommended Prereq: CHEM 342 . {Also offered for graduate credit - see BIOC 660 .}.

title: Foundations of Biochemistry I Laboratory
description: Laboratory to accompany BIOC 460 .  Introduction to techniques and instrumentation in biochemistry.  Co-Req: BIOC 460 .

title: Foundations of Biochemistry and Molecular Biology II
description: Interrelations between metabolic pathways and controls, with emphasis on mammalian systems; biochemistry of specialized tissues, fluids, and hormones, regulation of gene expression in eukaryotes; genetic defects in metabolism. 3 lectures. Recommended prereq: BIOC 460 . {Also offered for graduate credit - see BIOC 661 .}.

title: Methods of Biochemical Research
description: Advanced separation, characterization, and enzymological techniques for research in the biological sciences are emphasized. 1 lecture, 2 three-hour laboratories. Prereq: BIOC 460 . {Also offered for graduate credit - see BIOC 673 .}.

title: Methods of Recombinant DNA Technology
description: Principles and techniques of recombinant DNA construction, gene cloning, and analysis of gene structure. 1 lecture, 2 three-hour laboratories. Prereq: BIOC 460 . Co-req: BIOC 461 . Recommended: ZOO 315. {Also offered for graduate credit - see BIOC 674 .}.

title: Computer Applications in Biochemistry and Molecular Biology
description: This course will cover basic and advanced biochemical calculations and the use of computer programs to make these calculations. Programs for the presentation of data and seminars will also be presented. Prereq: BIOC 460 . {Also offered for graduate credit - see BIOC 675 .}.

title: Cellular Signal Transduction Processes and Metabolic Regulations
description: Advanced topics in regulation of metabolic processes including signal transduction, reversible and irreversible covalent modification, hormonal effects, protein turnover, and related phenomena. 2 lectures. {Also offered for graduate credit - see BIOC 683 .}.

title: Molecular Biology of Gene Expression
description: This is an advanced undergraduate course designed to analyze current information regarding biochemistry and molecular biology of gene expression and regulation in prokaryoates, eukaryoates and archea, with primary emphasis on eukaryotic systems. Prereq: BIOC 460 , BIOC 461 .

title: Global Practicum: Study Abroad
description: Pre-Arranged study at accredited foreign institutions (study abroad), domestic institutions (National Student Exchange) or on approved study abroad programs. Pre-requisite: Sophomore standing and prior approval by International Student and Study Abroad Services and major department. Graded 'P'or 'F' (Undergraduate), or 'S' or 'U' (Graduate).

description: This course will cover basic and advanced biochemical calculations and the use of computer programs to make these calculations. Programs for the presentation of data and seminars will also be presented. Prereq: BIOC 660 . {Also offered for undergraduate credit - see BIOC 475 .}.

description: Advanced topics in regulation of metabolic processes including signal transduction, reversible and irreversible covalent modification, hormonal effects, protein turnover, and related phenomena. 2 lectures. Prereq: BIOC 702 . F (alternate years) {Also offered for undergraduate credit - see BIOC 483 .}.

title: Comprehensive Biochemistry I
description: Comprehensive treatment of the chemistry and biochemistry of proteins, nucleic acids, carbohydrates, lipids, vitamins, hormones, and the specific metabolism of these substances. 4 lectures.

title: Comprehensive Biochemistry II
description: Comprehensive treatment of the chemistry and biochemistry of proteins, nucleic acids, carbohydrates, lipids, vitamins, hormones, and the specific metabolism of these substances. 4 lectures. Recommended: BIOC 701 .

title: Protein and Enzyme Biochemistry
description: Advanced topics in protein properties and structure, and the influence of these factors on enzyme kinetics and mechanism. 3 lectures. Prereq: BIOC 702 . S (alternate years).

title: Molecular Biology of Gene Expression and Regulation
description: Advanced topics in molecular biology and regulation in prokaryotes, eukaryotes, and archaea; early events in developmental gene expression. 3 lectures. Prereq: BIOC 702 . F (alternate years).

title: Structural Basis of Membrane Transport and Signaling
description: Advanced topics discussing how three-dimensional structures of membrane proteins dictate their function in coordinating the extracellular environment with intracellular processes. Prereq: BIOC 660 or BIOC 701 .

description: A group of students engaged, under a professor or professors, in research or criticism and in presentation of reports pertaining thereto.

If a student holding an F-1 or J-1 visa needs to enroll in an 898 course, they must receive prior approval to do so from the international student advisor in Global Programs and Services. Graded S/U. Requires department approval.

title: Basic Skills in Leadership and Personal Marketing
description: This course challenges students to begin the process of developing their leadership abilities and then developing the skills to promote their ideas and persuade others in personal and professional contexts. Grounded in an understanding of the art of leading and influence in various cultures and professions, students will assess their own leadership characteristics, analyze and reflect on leadership in everyday life, and then develop a plan and skills to persuasively present themselves and ideas to others, both personally and professionally. Open to all majors.

title: Introduction to Hospitality Business
description: This course provides an overview of the hospitality business, covering its history, components, career opportunities, development, and future trends. Applications to hospitality, such as foodservice, lodging, and travel, are explored. This course is open to students pursuing any major.

title: Skills for Academic Success
description: Development of skills and techniques for academic success. Includes study techniques, time management, test taking, note taking, goal setting, wellness, stress management, and career orientation. Introduction to campus resources and governance.

title: Seminar
description: A group of students engaged, under a professor or professors, in research or criticism and in presentation of reports pertaining thereto.

title: World Culture and Food Service
description: This course is an exploration of a variety of cultures and foods that demonstrate the influences of demography, geography, history, social traditions, religious beliefs, and other environmental considerations on food, its preparation, service, and symbolism/meaning. This course is open to students pursuing any major.

title: Introduction To Business
description: This course provides an overview of the environment of business, legal forms of business, and the major functional areas of business including management, marketing, accounting, and finance. This course is open to students pursuing any major.

title: Seminar
description: A group of students engaged, under a professor or professors, in research or criticism and in presentation of reports pertaining thereto.

title: Global Practicum: Study Abroad
description: Pre-Arranged study at accredited foreign institutions (study abroad), domestic institutions (National Student Exchange) or on approved study abroad programs. Pre-requisite: Sophomore standing and prior approval by International Student and Study Abroad Services and major department. Graded 'P'or 'F' (Undergraduate), or 'S' or 'U' (Graduate).

title: Taxation in Management Decisions
description: Study of the fundamental concepts of tax implications that result from common business transactions. Restricted to College of Business major or minor and a 2.50 minimum NDSU grade point average. Prereq: ACCT 102 or ACCT 201 . Cross-listed with ACCT 318 .

title: International Business
description: Study of international business: ways in which it differs from domestic operations; benefits of operating globally; and  political, cultural, and economic problems faced by managers of firms engaged in international activities.  Restricted to College of Business major or minor and a 2.50 minimum NDSU grade point average.

title: Global Business Environment
description: Introduction to the policies, procedures, and institutions impacting the global business environment, including visits to relevant businesses, institutions, and historical sites. This course is taught exclusively as part of an NDSU study abroad experience. Prereq: acceptance into the NDSU College of Business program in Europe. May be repeated for credit.

title: Principles of Real Estate
description: Principles and techniques of real estate appraisals, practical application of appraisal principles, and techniques to real property evaluation. Students must be College of Business major or minor and a 2.50 minimum NDSU grade point average. Cross-listed with AGEC 347 .

title: Faculty-Led Study Abroad
description: NDSU instructed experience or field study in a foreign country. Conducted in English for NDSU resident credit. Requires Global Programs and Services, and departmental approval. May be repeated for credit.

title: Analytics for Business Decision Making
description:
Focuses on empowering students with advanced Excel skills for business analytics, enhancing their capability to analyze and interpret data effectively. The course blends theoretical knowledge with practical application, teaching students to use analytical tools to make data-driven decisions that align with business strategies. This hands-on approach prepares students for real-world business challenges, enabling them to contribute to business insights and decision-making in professional settings. No mathematical background beyond high school algebra is required for an understanding of the material.
Prereq or Coreq: College of Business major, minor or certificate or BS in Data Science and a 2.50 minimum NDSU grade point average.

title: Organizational Communication I
description: Exploration of the theory of management communication practices in organizations. Emphasis on the formal structure and interpersonal aspects of supervisor-subordinate relations. Prereq: Students must be College of Business major or minor and a 2.50 minimum NDSU grade point average. Cross-listed with COMM.

title: Seminar
description: Restricted to College of Business major or minor and a 2.50 minimum NDSU grade point average.

title: Global Practicum: Study Abroad
description: Pre-Arranged study at accredited foreign institutions (study abroad), domestic institutions (National Student Exchange) or on approved study abroad programs. Pre-requisite: Sophomore standing and prior approval by International Student and Study Abroad Services and major department. Graded 'P'or 'F' (Undergraduate), or 'S' or 'U' (Graduate).

title: Individual Study
description: Restricted to College of Business major or minor and a 2.50 minimum NDSU grade point average.

title: Business Internship
description: Supervised professional experience with an appropriate public or private business. Students must meet standards established by the employer and the College of Business.  Restricted to College of Business major or minor, Jr or Sr classification, and a 2.50 minimum NDSU grade point average.

title: Legal and Social Environment of Business
description: Study of legal and regulatory environment in which business firms operate, as well as the social environment. Includes business ethics and social responsibility issues.  Restricted to College of Business major or minor and a 2.50 minimum NDSU grade point average. {Also offered for graduate credit - see BUSN 630.}.

title: Business Law I-Contracts, Property and Torts
description: A study of the foundations of business law and commercial transactions: the law of contracts, personal property, real estate, insurance, wills and estates, and torts.  Restricted to College of Business major or minor and a 2.50 minimum NDSU grade point average.

title: Cooperatives
description: Theory, practice, and evaluation of cooperatives including principles, management, marketing, finance, taxes, legal issues, and adjusting to change. Prereq: ECON 201 . Cross-listed with AGEC 474 . {Also offered for graduate credit - see BUSN 674 .}.

title: Strategic Management
description:
Analysis of strategic business issues, and integration of business-related knowledge and cross-functional business principles through experiential exercise. Capstone for all College of Business majors.
Prereq: FIN 320 , MGMT 320 , MRKT 320 .
Co-req: BUSN 430 .
Prereq or Coreq: Senior Standing.

description: Pre-Arranged study at accredited foreign institutions (study abroad), domestic institutions (National Student Exchange) or on approved study abroad programs. Pre-requisite: Sophomore standing and prior approval by International Student and Study Abroad Services and major department. Graded 'P'or 'F' (Undergraduate), or 'S' or 'U' (Graduate).

description: Restricted to College of Business major or minor and a 2.50 minimum NDSU grade point average.

title: Field Experience
description: Restricted to College of Business professional major or minor and a 2.50 minimum NDSU grade point average.

title: Special Topics
description: Restricted to College of Business major or minor and a 2.50 minimum NDSU grade point average.
